transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S
SOURCE: Newsweek

ARTICLE:

By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S. President Donald Trump has said the U.S. is "seeking immediate negotiations" to acquire Greenland, deepening the yawning chasm opening up between Washington and its closest allies over the Arctic territory.Trump called Greenland "part of North America" and "our territory" during a rambling speech to world leaders at the World Economic Forum in the Swiss town of Davos on Wednesday.The Republican reiterated his claim that the U.S. is the only power that can protect the mostly ice-covered expanse, and insisted this would not pose a threat to NATO.Alliance officials have repeatedly said a U.S. military attack on Greenland, a semi-autonomous part of Denmark, would spell the end of NATO. The prospect of the U.S. — the most influential and powerful member of NATO —attacking territory belonging to a fellow alliance member has always been unfathomable, until now.The White House had refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, which would be a violation of international law."I don't want to use force. I won't use force," Trump said. "All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland."This is a breaking story. More to follow....Request Reprint & LicensingSubmit CorrectionView Editorial & AI GuidelinesAdd Newsweek as a preferred source on Google to see more of our trusted coverage when you search.Related PodcastsTop StoriesFor MembersWorldWhy the US Won't Be Coming to Its Longtime Ally's Aid in Syria10 min readFor MembersNewsUncommon Knowledge: China Complaining About Trump's Land Grab? Newsweek Contributors Debate6 min readOpinionSyria's Kurds Lose Out | Opinion6 min readOpinionAgriculture Secretary: A Year of Results—How the Trump Admin Put American Farmers First | Opinion6 min readOpinionThe New American ICE Age | Opinion5 min readFor MembersOpinionConventional Wisdom: Greenland Edition3 min read
---

{
  "summary": "In a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the United States is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, intensifying tensions between Washington and its NATO allies—particularly Denmark, of which Greenland is a semi-autonomous part. Trump described Greenland as \"part of North America\" and referred to it as \"our territory,\" emphasizing that the U.S. is the only nation capable of protecting the ice-covered island. This stance has alarmed NATO officials, who warned that any U.S. military attempt to seize Greenland would likely spell the end of the NATO alliance, given that it would constitute an attack on a member’s territory. Despite prior refusals by the White House to rule out military force in the acquisition, Trump explicitly stated, \"I don't want to use force. I won't use force,\" clarifying that the U.S. aims only to secure Greenland through negotiations. The situation remains unresolved, with international and alliance norms hanging in the balance as the story unfolds.",

    "The White House previously would not rule out force, but Trump said he won’t use it.",
    "Greenland is a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark and a NATO member's land."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "U.S. government/Trump",
    "NATO officials",
    "Danish/Greenlandic perspective"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: In a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the United States is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, intensifying tensions between Washington and its NATO allies—particularly Denmark, of which Greenland is a semi-autonomous part. Trump described Greenland as "part of North America" and referred to it as "our territory," emphasizing that the U.S. is the only nation capable of protecting the ice-covered island. This stance has alarmed NATO officials, who warned that any U.S. military attempt to seize Greenland would likely spell the end of the NATO alliance, given that it would constitute an attack on a member’s territory. Despite prior refusals by the White House to rule out military force in the acquisition, Trump explicitly stated, "I don't want to use force. I won't use force," clarifying that the U.S. aims only to secure Greenland through negotiations. The situation remains unresolved, with international and alliance norms hanging in the balance as the story unfolds.
key_facts: President Trump declared the U.S. is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland.
Trump described Greenland as 'part of North America' and 'our territory' at Davos.
NATO officials warned that a U.S. military move on Greenland would end the alliance.
The White House previously would not rule out force, but Trump said he won’t use it.
Greenland is a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark and a NATO member's land.
